---
title: "About"
toc: true
---

::: {.hero}
::: {.kicker}
ABOUT THIS CURRICULUM
:::
# About
::: {.lead}
A standalone, open curriculum for people who would rather understand
statistics than memorise recipes.
:::
:::

## Purpose

The aim of this site is to offer a generous, opinionated biostatistics
curriculum to the biomedical research community — the kind of curriculum
the authors wish they had been handed at the start of their own PhDs.
It covers the statistical territory a working biomedical researcher needs
to read papers critically, design studies defensibly, analyse their own
data, and write up results honestly. The material runs from first
principles through modern statistical learning, and it is freely available
for self-study, for citation, and for adaptation into local teaching.

## Philosophy

Three convictions run through every lab.

**Statistics is thinking, not buttons.** The hardest part of any analysis
is not which function to call but whether the question, the design, and the
data are good enough to answer it. We therefore begin every inference lab
with a hypothesis and a plot, not with a line of code. When the thinking
is clear the test almost writes itself; when the thinking is muddled no
amount of output will rescue it.

**Reproducibility is the default, not a decoration.** Every lab renders
from a Quarto source, every package version is pinned with `renv`, every
figure is generated by the same code that fitted the model, and every
dataset is either built into R or simulated in the file that uses it.
If the site cannot be re-rendered from a clean clone, we consider it
broken.

**If you cannot present it, you do not yet understand it.** Every lab
in the curriculum renders twice — once as a long-form article and once as
a Reveal.js slide deck. The act of boiling a method down to eight slides
is a reliable test of comprehension, and the deck becomes a gift you
can share the moment your department asks who would like to run Friday's
seminar.

## Licence

The curriculum is released under the MIT licence. You are free to use,
modify, and redistribute the material, including in a commercial or
institutional teaching context, provided you retain the copyright notice
and the acknowledgements below.

## Authorship

*Primary author: to be filled in by the repository owner.*

Contributions, corrections, and suggestions are welcome via pull request
or issue on the project's GitHub repository.

## Acknowledgements

Heartfelt thanks to **Chi Zhang** and the OCBE team at the University of
Oslo for their open-source course
[*MF9130E — Introductory Course in Statistics*](https://ocbe-uio.github.io/teaching_mf9130e/)
and the accompanying [GitHub repository](https://github.com/ocbe-uio/teaching_mf9130e).
Their clarity and commitment to making statistics teaching openly
accessible was the direct inspiration for this site. See
[References](references.qmd) for the full list of sources.
